summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Evaluation of hypogonadism and concerns regarding gynecomastia.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports being referred for hypogonadism and concerns regarding gynecomastia. He states he has been managing these conditions for many years and feels generally well. He reports feeling more vigorous since starting testosterone replacement therapy, sleeping better, and experiencing positive effects. He takes finasteride at half a pill (5mg) and cialis daily, adjusting the dose of cialis based on his work schedule. He currently takes 140mg of testosterone and reports feeling that his levels are high. He is taking half a 5mg finasteride pill and 2.5mg of cialis on days he works out. He prefers paper prescriptions. He is currently taking testosterone at a dose of 140 milligrams. He is taking finasteride. He is taking cialis daily.

Findings
Physical exam reveals no acute distress. Heart and lungs are clear to auscultation. Cholesterol was measured and is 5 months later. PSA level is 0.6. The patient's red blood cell count has increased. The patient reports pain in his left hand.

Assessment
The patient is diagnosed with hypogonadism and gynecomastia. His testosterone levels are elevated. His blood thickness is at increased risk.

Plan
The doctor will not change the patient's dose. We will take it. The doctor will get the prescriptions. The patient requests to increase his testosterone dosage to 175 milligrams. Follow up in four weeks if symptoms persist. Order shoulder radiographs. Initiate physical therapy. Continue acetaminophen as needed.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Hypogonadism.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Paul Edwards is a 59-year-old male, an established patient, who presents to the clinic today for hypogonadism. He was last seen on 11/24/2020. The patient’s history includes positive for HIV, and today he is concerned with gynecomastia.

The patient is doing well, overall, and feels the testosterone is helping. He reports weight stability, feels lively, good, more vigorous, and he is sleeping well. He also thinks the testosterone is having a positive effect on his blood work. He endorses that his triglycerides have always been elevated and adds that they were as high as 265 mg/dL. The patient presented a copy of his bloodwork, showing a significant decrease in his cholesterol after 5 months. Mr. Edwards questioned if the changes in his blood work were due to the testosterone. He also noted that his red blood cell count has not increased. The patient inquired about possible benefits of Indole-3-carbinol, which is a broccoli extract for estrogen control.

Currently, the patient is dosing 140 ng/dL of testosterone per week and inquired if his dose could be increased to 175 ng/dL. He recalled that he had recently injected testosterone before his last testosterone blood work was performed; last testosterone levels were greater than 1500 ng/dL. The patient confirms continued daily use of Finasteride and Cialis, prescribed by me, and stated he needs a refill for both.

Mr. Edwards mentioned he is taking correct steps to get his life "together." He also uses a med calendar to help with medication compliance.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
HIV

SOCIAL HISTORY

Utilizes med calendar to support medication compliance.
Actively trying to get his life together.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Cialis 5 mg tablet by mouth on days he exercises, 2.5 mg tablet by mouth on days without exercise.
Finasteride 5 mg half tablet daily.
Testosterone cypionate 140 mg.

RESULTS

PSA 0.6 ng/mL.
Triglycerides 145 mg/dL.
Total testosterone 1500 ng/dL, 11/24/2021.

ASSESSMENT

• Hypogonadism.

PLAN

I recommend maintaining current management. The patient’s last testosterone levels were greater than 1500 ng/dL, although this level was likely related to his recent injection. He requested an increase to his testosterone cypionate from 140 mg to 175 mg, which I denied due to his already high testosterone levels. I counseled the patient on the risks associated with high testosterone levels and Indole-3-carbinol and it’s lacking evidence to support his estrogen levels. The last time his estradiol levels were checked, they were normal; recommend annual screening.

I provided paper prescription refills for Cialis and Finasteride.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain in his left hand.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported symptom rather than an exam or test finding.

section_miscategorizations.text: Cholesterol was measured and is 5 months later.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This appears to describe timing/history of prior cholesterol measurement rather than a clear current result; it reads more like historical context than a present objective finding.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient requests to increase his testosterone dosage to 175 milligrams.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is the patient's stated preference/request, which fits better in the history; it is not clearly an agreed-upon management step.

This is a diagnostic imaging order that does not relate to the stated hypogonadism/gynecomastia assessment and appears out of context; without any shoulder-related assessment, it is likely misplaced from a different encounter.
This therapeutic order is unrelated to the documented assessment (hypogonadism and gynecomastia) and appears to belong to a different problem/visit, suggesting misplacement from another note.

This analgesic instruction is not connected to any problem in the assessment (no pain condition is assessed), suggesting it is carried over from another context and miscategorized for this focused endocrine visit.
